heroOverview: Pouchitis is inflammation of the internal ileal pouch-anal anastomosis (IPAA) — a surgically created reservoir constructed from the small intestine after removal of the colon and rectum, most commonly performed for ulcerative colitis. It is the most frequent long-term complication of IPAA surgery, affecting approximately 50% of pouch patients within ten years of their operation. The hallmark symptoms are increased stool frequency, urgency, watery diarrhoea, pelvic pain, and rectal bleeding, caused by bacterial dysbiosis and inflammatory infiltration of the pouch mucosa. In its acute form, pouchitis responds rapidly — typically within one to two weeks — to a short course of antibiotics such as metronidazole or ciprofloxacin. However, around 10-15% of patients develop chronic or refractory pouchitis that requires long-term therapy and, in a minority, pouch excision.

keyStats.prevalence: Affects approximately 50% of IPAA patients within 10 years of surgery; annual incidence is 7-20% per pouch-year
keyStats.demographics: Exclusively occurs in patients who have undergone IPAA (pouch) surgery, predominantly for ulcerative colitis; men appear slightly more affected than women
keyStats.avgAge: Most cases present in adults aged 25-55, corresponding to the post-surgical cohort with ulcerative colitis
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 30,000-50,000 IPAA procedures are performed annually worldwide; a large proportion of these patients will experience at least one episode of pouchitis

definition: Pouchitis (ICD-10: K91.850) is inflammation of the ileal pouch created after proctocolectomy and ileal pouch-anal anastomosis (IPAA), diagnosed by a combination of clinical symptoms, endoscopic findings, and histological evidence of acute inflammation in the pouch mucosa. It is the most common pouch-specific complication and a major determinant of long-term pouch function and quality of life. The pathophysiology centres on dysbiosis of the dense luminal microbiome within the pouch — which, unlike normal small bowel, develops a colonic-like flora under stasis — triggering a mucosal inflammatory response. Pouchitis is classified as acute (less than 4 weeks, responds to first-line antibiotics), chronic (greater than 4 weeks or more than three episodes per year), antibiotic-responsive, antibiotic-dependent, or antibiotic-refractory, a classification that guides escalating treatment. It falls within the gastroenterology specialty, with a specific role for a gastroenterologist experienced in pouch disease, ideally working alongside the original colorectal surgeon. A validated Pouchitis Disease Activity Index (PDAI) scoring symptoms, endoscopy, and histology is used in research and increasingly in clinical follow-up to standardise assessment.

typesClassification.description: A first or isolated episode lasting under 4 weeks; responds in approximately 85-90% of cases to a 10-14 day course of oral antibiotics, with complete symptom resolution.
Three or more acute episodes per year; patients respond to each antibiotic course but require repeated treatment and eventually prophylaxis.
Continuous or near-continuous antibiotic therapy is required to maintain remission; cessation causes prompt relapse within days to weeks.
Fails to respond to standard oral antibiotic courses; requires biologics, immunomodulators, or consideration of pouch excision.
Caused by NSAIDs, ischaemia, Clostridioides difficile, or CMV infection; directed treatment of the underlying cause is key.
Approximately 5-10% of IPAA patients develop Crohn-like features — structuring, fistulae, perianal disease — often mis-labelled as pouchitis; this distinction changes management fundamentally.

primarySymptoms: Increased bowel frequency — typically six or more liquid stools per day compared to the patient's usual baseline after IPAA surgery.
Urgency to defecate that may be difficult to defer, sometimes accompanied by faecal incontinence.
Watery or loose stools rather than the firmer output expected from a well-functioning pouch.
Rectal bleeding or blood in the stool, reflecting inflamed and friable pouch mucosa.
Pelvic or lower abdominal cramping pain or discomfort, often preceding defecation.
Nocturnal defecation — being woken from sleep to use the toilet, a sensitive marker of active pouch inflammation.
Faecal incontinence during active episodes, particularly at night, due to sphincter fatigue and high stool volumes.
Tenesmus — a persistent sensation of rectal fullness and the urge to defecate even when the pouch is empty.
Systemic symptoms including fever, malaise, and fatigue in moderate-to-severe episodes.
Extraintestinal manifestations — joint aches, skin rashes, or eye inflammation — which can accompany active pouchitis in patients with a background of IBD.
earlyWarningSigns: A gradual increase in daily bowel movements above the patient's established post-operative baseline.
Stools becoming more liquid or unformed over several days after a period of good function.
Nocturnal urgency beginning after weeks of undisturbed sleep.
Mild perianal soreness or irritation heralding mucosal inflammation before frank bleeding.
emergencySigns: Fever above 38.5°C with severe pelvic pain and high-volume diarrhoea in a pouch patient — may indicate severe or complicated pouchitis, Clostridioides difficile infection, or abscess.
Bright red rectal bleeding with volume sufficient to cause dizziness or anaemia.
Sudden severe perineal pain with swelling or visible perineal mass — possible pouch abscess or fistula.
Complete failure to defecate despite urgency combined with abdominal distension — possible pouch outlet obstruction.

causes.description: After IPAA, the small intestinal segment forming the pouch is colonised by a dense, colonic-type bacterial flora; abnormal shifts in microbiome composition trigger innate immune activation, epithelial barrier dysfunction, and mucosal inflammation in genetically predisposed individuals.

Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs impair the mucosal barrier of the pouch lining by inhibiting prostaglandin synthesis, increasing permeability and allowing luminal bacteria to drive inflammation; regular NSAID use is associated with a two- to threefold increase in pouchitis risk.

C. difficile can colonise and cause toxin-mediated mucosal damage in the pouch, precipitating antibiotic-refractory pouchitis or severe flares in a patient otherwise in remission; stool testing for C. difficile toxin is mandatory when pouchitis fails initial treatment.

In immunosuppressed pouch patients or those with refractory pouchitis on biologic therapy, CMV reactivation causes ulceration of the pouch mucosa visible on biopsy; directed antiviral therapy with ganciclovir is required.

Relative ischaemia from mesenteric tension or angulation at the time of IPAA construction, or from progressive vascular insufficiency, results in a distinct ischaemic pouchitis pattern with chronic non-healing ulceration and poor response to antibiotics.

Variants in innate immune genes including NOD2 and ATG16L1 are associated with higher pouchitis risk, mirroring their role in Crohn's disease susceptibility and suggesting a shared pathophysiology in genetically vulnerable individuals.

riskFactors.description: Pouchitis is almost exclusive to UC patients; those who had IPAA for familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) have pouchitis rates below 5%, highlighting the inflammatory rather than purely mechanical origin in UC patients.

riskFactors.factor: Extensive UC and backwash ileitis pre-operatively
riskFactors.description: Patients with pancolitis and backwash ileitis — small bowel inflammation from reflux of colonic contents before colectomy — have approximately twice the pouchitis rate of those with distal disease only.

Perinuclear antineutrophil cytoplasmic antibody (p-ANCA) positivity before surgery predicts a higher rate of chronic and refractory pouchitis; it is present in 60-70% of UC patients and serves as a risk stratifier.
After a first pouchitis episode, approximately 50% of patients relapse within one year; after a second, over 70% have ongoing disease, making prior episodes the strongest predictor of recurrence.
NSAIDs should be avoided in IPAA patients where possible; paracetamol (acetaminophen) is the preferred analgesic for minor pain in this population.

Unlike ulcerative colitis itself (paradoxically protected by smoking), pouchitis is more common in non-smokers; the mechanism is poorly understood but nicotinic receptor effects on mucosal immunity have been proposed.
Pouchitis incidence rises with time after surgery; cumulative risk reaches 50% at 10 years, and most first episodes occur within the first two years of pouch creation.

affectedDemographics: Adults who have undergone IPAA surgery for ulcerative colitis — the only patient population at risk
Patients with extensive or severe pre-operative UC and those with pre-operative backwash ileitis
Young to middle-aged adults aged 25-55, reflecting the typical age of UC surgery
Males slightly more often than females, though the difference is modest
IBD patients with genetic markers such as p-ANCA positivity or NOD2 variants
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of pouchitis requires a combination of clinical symptoms, endoscopic findings, and histological confirmation — the three domains captured by the Pouchitis Disease Activity Index (PDAI). Clinically, increased stool frequency above the patient's established post-operative baseline combined with urgency and liquid stools is the trigger for investigation. Pouchoscopy — flexible endoscopy of the pouch — is the definitive diagnostic procedure, revealing diffuse mucosal erythema, oedema, loss of vascular pattern, contact bleeding, erosions, and ulceration in active pouchitis; biopsies showing acute inflammatory infiltrate confirm the histological criterion. Importantly, pouchoscopy also excludes Crohn's disease of the pouch (which causes fistulae, structuring, or perianal involvement) and detects secondary causes such as CMV inclusions on biopsy or cuffitis (inflammation of the residual rectal cuff just below the pouch-anal anastomosis, which requires separate treatment). Stool culture and Clostridioides difficile toxin assay are mandatory in patients who fail first-line antibiotics. Blood tests may show raised CRP and a neutrophilia in moderate-to-severe episodes; faecal calprotectin is elevated and correlates with endoscopic activity, making it a useful non-invasive monitoring tool.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive endoscopic and histological diagnosis of pouchitis; distinguishes acute pouchitis from Crohn's disease of the pouch, cuffitis, and secondary causes; quantifies severity via the PDAI endoscopic subscore.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A flexible endoscope is passed via the anus into the pouch; small tissue samples are taken from the mucosa. Sedation is usually offered. The procedure takes 15-30 minutes and mild bloating is common afterwards.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A validated 18-point scoring system combining symptom score (0-6), endoscopic score (0-6), and histological score (0-6); a total score above 7 defines active pouchitis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Calculated from questionnaire data, endoscopy findings, and biopsy results at each clinic visit; not a separate test but an integrated score.

diagnosticTests.test: Stool culture and Clostridioides difficile toxin assay
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies infectious triggers or secondary causes of pouchitis, particularly C. difficile or CMV, especially when antibiotic treatment fails.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A stool sample is collected and sent to the laboratory; results take 24-72 hours.

diagnosticTests.purpose: A non-invasive marker of intestinal inflammation; elevated (typically above 150 µg/g) in active pouchitis and useful for monitoring response to treatment and detecting relapse between scopes.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small stool sample is collected and posted to the laboratory; results available within 3-5 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses systemic inflammation, anaemia from blood loss, and nutritional status in moderate-to-severe or chronic pouchitis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard arm blood draw with results within 2-4 hours; repeated at intervals during treatment.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates for complications of pouchitis or Crohn's disease of the pouch, including pelvic abscess, fistulae, structuring, and pouch-vaginal fistula.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie in an MRI scanner for 30-45 minutes; no radiation or contrast injection is usually needed.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of pouchitis is stratified by disease pattern and response to prior therapy. For a first or isolated acute episode, oral ciprofloxacin 500 mg twice daily for 14 days is the preferred first-line antibiotic, achieving clinical remission in approximately 85-90% of patients with symptom improvement apparent within three to five days. Metronidazole 400 mg three times daily is an effective alternative where ciprofloxacin is unavailable or contraindicated, though it carries a higher risk of nausea, metallic taste, and peripheral neuropathy with prolonged use. For relapsing pouchitis (two or more episodes per year), acute episodes are treated with antibiotics as above, followed by maintenance prophylaxis using VSL#3 high-dose probiotics or low-dose continuous ciprofloxacin to prevent recurrence. Antibiotic-dependent pouchitis requires continuous or rotating antibiotic regimens; rotation across metronidazole, ciprofloxacin, rifaximin, and tetracycline reduces cumulative side-effect burden. Antibiotic-refractory pouchitis is managed with oral budesonide at 9 mg daily, topical mesalamine enemas, or biological agents: vedolizumab (a gut-selective anti-integrin biologic) has the strongest evidence base, and anti-TNF agents (infliximab, adalimumab) are also used. Ustekinumab targeting the IL-12/23 pathway is an emerging option. Before escalating to biologics, secondary causes including C. difficile infection, CMV reactivation, and NSAID-induced mucosal injury must always be identified and specifically treated.

medicalTreatments.name: Ciprofloxacin (first-line antibiotic)
medicalTreatments.description: A fluoroquinolone antibiotic given orally at 500 mg twice daily for 14 days; preferred over metronidazole as first-line because of equivalent efficacy and lower rates of nausea, metallic taste, and peripheral neuropathy.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Induces remission in approximately 85-90% of acute pouchitis episodes; a randomised trial showed superiority over metronidazole with remission rates of 82% versus 61% at 14 days.

medicalTreatments.name: Metronidazole (first-line antibiotic)
medicalTreatments.description: An oral antibiotic effective in acute pouchitis; widely used where ciprofloxacin is not available or tolerated; carries risks of nausea, metallic taste, and peripheral neuropathy with prolonged use.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves remission in approximately 60-70% of acute episodes; effective as combination therapy with ciprofloxacin in antibiotic-dependent cases.

medicalTreatments.name: VSL#3 probiotic (high-dose probiotic blend)
medicalTreatments.description: A preparation containing eight bacterial strains at very high concentrations; used as maintenance therapy after antibiotic-induced remission in relapsing pouchitis to prevent further episodes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Randomised trials show VSL#3 maintains remission in approximately 85% of patients with antibiotic-responsive relapsing pouchitis versus 0% on placebo.

medicalTreatments.name: Vedolizumab (anti-integrin biologic)
medicalTreatments.description: A gut-selective monoclonal antibody blocking α4β7 integrin, reducing lymphocyte trafficking to the intestine; the evidence base for vedolizumab in chronic refractory pouchitis is strongest among biologics.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Observational studies and the EARNEST randomised trial showed vedolizumab achieved clinical response in approximately 30-50% of antibiotic-refractory pouchitis patients at 14 weeks.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral budesonide or mesalamine enemas
medicalTreatments.description: Budesonide (a topical steroid with low systemic absorption) given orally at 9 mg daily, or mesalamine enemas applied locally, are used for mild antibiotic-refractory disease or as steroid-sparing bridges.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Budesonide induces short-term clinical improvement in approximately 50-60% of antibiotic-refractory cases; sustained benefit beyond 3 months is less well established.

surgicalOptions.name: Pouch excision with permanent ileostomy
surgicalOptions.description: Removal of the ileal pouch and construction of a permanent end ileostomy; the last resort for medically refractory pouchitis or pouch failure from Crohn's disease, stricture, or fistula. Approximately 5-10% of IPAA patients ultimately require pouch excision.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Eliminates pouchitis by definition; quality of life with a well-managed permanent ileostomy is comparable to a failed pouch, and many patients report significant improvement after pouch removal.

alternativeTreatments.name: Faecal microbiota transplantation (FMT)
alternativeTreatments.description: Infusion of donor faecal microbiome into the pouch via endoscopy; small trials show modest benefit in antibiotic-refractory pouchitis, improving microbial diversity, though evidence is still limited and protocols are unstandardised.

alternativeTreatments.name: Bismuth subsalicylate enemas
alternativeTreatments.description: Topical bismuth has antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties in the pouch; used as an adjunct in antibiotic-dependent pouchitis, particularly in patients concerned about antibiotic overuse.

alternativeTreatments.name: Dietary interventions (low-FODMAP diet)
alternativeTreatments.description: A low-fermentable carbohydrate diet reduces luminal bacterial fermentation and gas production; it can improve stool consistency and reduce urgency in stable patients, though it does not treat active inflammation and should not replace antibiotic therapy during a flare.

whySeeSpecialist: All patients who have had IPAA surgery should have ongoing gastroenterology follow-up, particularly in the first two years when pouchitis risk is highest. Referral to a gastroenterologist experienced in pouch disease is critical when the pattern becomes chronic, refractory, or complicated by Crohn-like features or fistulae. Specialist input is also needed for biologics initiation and for the multidisciplinary decision about pouch salvage versus excision.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a gastroenterologist with specific experience in inflammatory bowel disease and ileal pouch disease, ideally at a centre that performed a high volume of IPAA procedures. These centres maintain dedicated pouch clinics with pouchoscopy capability, dietitian support, and close liaison with the colorectal surgeon who constructed the pouch. When biologics are being considered, a centre with an IBD biologics programme and access to clinical trials is valuable.
preventionStrategies: Avoid NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen, diclofenac) where possible; use paracetamol for pain relief.
Use VSL#3 probiotic maintenance therapy after a first or second pouchitis episode to prevent relapse.
Complete antibiotic courses fully and attend follow-up pouchoscopy as scheduled.
Report new symptoms promptly — early treatment of acute episodes prevents progression to chronic pouchitis.
Ensure C. difficile stool testing before escalating therapy; treating the correct organism avoids unnecessary immunosuppression.
Discuss ongoing tobacco cessation with your doctor — stopping smoking benefits overall health without substantially worsening pouch outcomes.
lifestyleModifications: Follow a low-FODMAP diet during acute flares if symptoms of urgency and bloating are dominant.
Identify and avoid individual food triggers (high-fibre foods, spicy meals) that worsen pouch output.
Stay well hydrated; IPAA patients have higher fluid requirements because the colon is absent.
Limit alcohol, which increases intestinal permeability and may worsen mucosal inflammation.
Carry a spare change of clothing and know the location of accessible toilets when out — urgency is unpredictable during a flare.
Practise pelvic floor exercises to improve sphincter tone and continence.

recommended: Adequate fluid intake — at least 2-2.5 litres daily — to compensate for reduced colonic water reabsorption
Low-FODMAP foods during active pouchitis to minimise gas, bloating, and fermentation-driven urgency
Plain, easily digestible foods (rice, boiled chicken, bananas) during acute flares
Psyllium or soluble fibre supplements to improve stool consistency and slow transit
Electrolyte solutions or sports drinks if high stool output causes sodium or potassium depletion
avoid: NSAIDs taken orally — the mucosal barrier effect directly triggers pouchitis flares
High-FODMAP foods (onions, garlic, legumes, wheat) during active inflammation
Excess alcohol and carbonated drinks that increase gut permeability and gas
Large meals that overwhelm pouch capacity and worsen urgency

exerciseGuidelines: Regular moderate exercise is safe and beneficial for overall wellbeing and immune regulation in IPAA patients. During an active pouchitis episode with high stool frequency and fatigue, reduce intensity to comfortable walking. Once in remission, return to full activity including sport is appropriate. High-intensity exercise during severe pouchitis may worsen urgency and incontinence.
dailyManagement: Monitor daily bowel frequency and consistency; track changes from your established post-operative baseline.
Take antibiotic courses precisely as prescribed, completing the full course even when symptoms improve by day three or four.
Take probiotic maintenance therapy (VSL#3 or equivalent) consistently if it has been prescribed for prevention.
Carry anti-diarrhoeal agents such as loperamide for temporary management of urgency, particularly at night.
Report any fever, blood in stools, or failure to improve on antibiotics within five days to your gastroenterologist.
Attend scheduled pouchoscopy and clinic reviews — most centres recommend annual scope after the first two years.
prognosis: For most IPAA patients, pouchitis is a manageable complication rather than a pouch-ending one. Acute single episodes respond to antibiotics in around 85-90% of cases without lasting consequences. Approximately 20-30% of patients develop recurrent or chronic pouchitis that requires ongoing treatment; among these, the large majority achieve sustained remission with antibiotics and probiotics. Antibiotic-refractory chronic pouchitis is more challenging, but biologic therapies now offer meaningful remission in 30-50% of cases. Around 5-10% of all IPAA patients ultimately require pouch excision due to failure from pouchitis or Crohn's disease of the pouch — a significant minority but the clear exception rather than the rule. Overall pouch survival at 10 years is approximately 85-90% in experienced centres.
recoveryTimeline: Acute pouchitis symptoms begin to improve within 48-72 hours of starting antibiotics in most patients and resolve by 10-14 days. Endoscopic and histological healing may lag behind symptom resolution by two to four weeks. Patients with chronic pouchitis on biological therapy typically see clinical response at four to six weeks and assess full response at three to six months. After pouch excision, recovery from surgery takes six to twelve weeks before a permanent ileostomy routine is established.
complications: Chronic antibiotic-dependent pouchitis — prolonged fluoroquinolone or metronidazole use risks peripheral neuropathy and tendinopathy, requiring antibiotic rotation and careful monitoring.

Crohn's disease of the pouch — approximately 5-10% of IPAA patients develop de novo Crohn's features including fistulae, structuring, and perianal abscesses, requiring a complete change in management strategy.
Pouch failure — severe pouchitis unresponsive to all therapies, together with stricture, fistula, or ischaemia, accounts for the 5-10% of IPAA patients who require permanent ileostomy.
Clostridioides difficile superinfection — can complicate antibiotic courses given to treat pouchitis, worsening the inflammatory milieu and requiring vancomycin or fidaxomicin.
Iron deficiency anaemia — chronic blood loss from inflamed mucosa and reduced iron absorption from a pouch depleted of distal small bowel contributes to anaemia requiring supplementation.

supportResources.name: Crohn's and Colitis Foundation — Pouchitis
supportResources.description: Patient-facing information on pouchitis causes, diagnosis, and treatment from the leading IBD patient advocacy organisation in the United States.

supportResources.name: NHS — Ileostomy, colostomy and pouch surgery
supportResources.description: UK NHS guidance on living with and managing complications after intestinal pouch surgery.

supportResources.name: Crohn's and Colitis UK — Pouchitis information sheet
supportResources.description: UK patient charity providing detailed information sheets on pouchitis and pouch disease for people living with IBD.

sources.title: Sandborn WJ et al. Pouchitis after ileal pouch-anal anastomosis: a Pouchitis Disease Activity Index. Mayo Clin Proc. 1994
Shen B et al. Diagnosis and classification of ileal pouch disorders. Nat Rev Gastroenterol Hepatol. 2022

ECCO Guidelines on Therapeutics in Ulcerative Colitis: Surgical Treatment. J Crohns Colitis. 2022

confusedWithConditions.name: Crohn's disease of the pouch
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Crohn's disease of the pouch causes fistulae, structuring, and perianal abscesses not seen in typical pouchitis; the distinction requires pouchoscopy with biopsy and MRI pelvis, and fundamentally changes treatment.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ulcerative colitis (pre-operative)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ulcerative colitis symptoms resemble pouchitis but occur in a native colon and rectum; pouchitis only occurs after IPAA surgery when the colon has been removed.

confusedWithConditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) of the pouch
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Functional symptoms resembling IBS can occur in IPAA patients with normal pouchoscopy; unlike pouchitis, there is no mucosal inflammation and antibiotics provide no benefit.

relatedConditions.name: Ulcerative colitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Pouchitis develops exclusively in patients who have had colectomy and IPAA for UC; the underlying IBD biology drives the pouch complication.

relatedConditions.name: Crohn's disease
relatedConditions.relevance: Crohn's disease of the pouch is the most important differential; patients initially diagnosed with UC may harbour Crohn's disease that declares itself only after pouch construction.

relatedConditions.name: Clostridioides difficile enterocolitis
relatedConditions.relevance: C. difficile is an important secondary cause of pouchitis and must be excluded in any antibiotic-refractory episode.

relatedConditions.name: IBS with diarrhoea
relatedConditions.relevance: Functional pouch symptoms without mucosal inflammation are managed differently from true pouchitis and must be distinguished by endoscopy.

faqs.question: What is pouchitis?
faqs.answer: Pouchitis is inflammation of the ileal pouch — a reservoir made from small intestine to restore continence after the colon and rectum are removed, most often for ulcerative colitis. It is the most common long-term complication of pouch surgery, affecting roughly 50% of patients within 10 years.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of pouchitis?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms are increased bowel frequency (six or more liquid stools per day), urgency, watery stools, rectal bleeding, pelvic cramping, and nocturnal defecation. Fever and fatigue occur in moderate to severe episodes. These symptoms differ from a patient's usual post-operative pouch function.

faqs.question: How is pouchitis treated?
faqs.answer: Acute pouchitis is treated with a 14-day course of oral antibiotics — ciprofloxacin (500 mg twice daily) is the preferred first choice, and metronidazole is an alternative. Roughly 85-90% of patients improve within one to two weeks. Recurrent or chronic cases may need probiotic maintenance, combination antibiotics, or biologic medicines.

faqs.question: How is pouchitis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires pouchoscopy (flexible endoscopy of the pouch) showing mucosal inflammation and biopsies confirming acute inflammatory changes on histology. The Pouchitis Disease Activity Index (PDAI) scores symptoms, endoscopy, and histology. Stool tests for C. difficile are added when antibiotics fail.

faqs.question: Will pouchitis damage my pouch permanently?
faqs.answer: A single or occasional episode of acute pouchitis, treated promptly with antibiotics, does not cause lasting pouch damage. Repeated or chronic untreated pouchitis can cause progressive mucosal scarring. Around 5-10% of all IPAA patients eventually require pouch removal, usually from chronic refractory disease or Crohn's disease of the pouch rather than simple acute pouchitis.

faqs.question: How long does pouchitis last?
faqs.answer: With appropriate antibiotic treatment, symptoms of acute pouchitis usually improve within 48-72 hours and resolve within 10-14 days. Patients with chronic or antibiotic-refractory pouchitis may have symptoms for weeks to months and require escalated therapy before remission is achieved.

faqs.question: Why does pouchitis keep coming back?
faqs.answer: Recurrent pouchitis affects roughly 50% of patients within a year of a first episode. The pouch's dense bacterial flora is inherently unstable in genetically susceptible people. Risk factors include having had extensive ulcerative colitis, p-ANCA positive blood tests, and regular NSAID use. Probiotic maintenance therapy with VSL#3 substantially reduces recurrence.

faqs.question: Is pouchitis the same as Crohn's disease?
faqs.answer: No, though distinguishing them is clinically critical. Classic pouchitis responds to antibiotics and shows diffuse mucosal inflammation on scope. Crohn's disease of the pouch causes fistulae, strictures, and perianal abscesses, does not respond to antibiotics alone, and requires biologic therapy. About 5-10% of IPAA patients develop Crohn's features after surgery.

faqs.question: Can NSAIDs cause pouchitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. NSAIDs such as ibuprofen, naproxen, and aspirin impair the mucosal barrier of the pouch lining and are associated with a two- to threefold increased risk of pouchitis. Pouch patients should use paracetamol (acetaminophen) for pain relief instead and avoid NSAIDs where possible.

faqs.question: What probiotics help pouchitis?
faqs.answer: VSL#3 is the best-studied probiotic for pouchitis prevention. It contains eight bacterial strains at high concentrations. In randomised trials, it maintained remission in approximately 85% of patients with relapsing pouchitis compared with 0% on placebo. It is used after antibiotic-induced remission, not during an active episode.

faqs.question: What is vedolizumab and when is it used for pouchitis?
faqs.answer: Vedolizumab is a biologic medicine that blocks immune cells from entering the gut. It is used for chronic antibiotic-refractory pouchitis — cases where metronidazole and ciprofloxacin no longer achieve remission. Clinical trials show it produces response in approximately 30-50% of refractory patients. It is given by intravenous infusion every eight weeks after induction.

faqs.question: What is the Pouchitis Disease Activity Index (PDAI)?
faqs.answer: The PDAI is an 18-point scoring tool used to define and grade pouchitis. It adds together a symptom score (bowel frequency, bleeding, urgency and cramping, fever — up to 6 points), an endoscopy score (mucosal appearance — up to 6 points), and a histology score (microscopic inflammation — up to 6 points). A total score of 7 or above defines active pouchitis.

faqs.question: Can C. difficile cause pouchitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Clostridioides difficile can colonise the ileal pouch and produce toxin-mediated inflammation indistinguishable from standard pouchitis. Stool C. difficile testing is mandatory when a patient fails initial antibiotic therapy. Treatment requires specific agents — oral vancomycin or fidaxomicin — not the usual pouchitis antibiotics.

faqs.question: Can pouchitis be cured?
faqs.answer: Acute pouchitis can be cured with antibiotics in the great majority of patients. Chronic pouchitis is not always curable but is manageable long-term with antibiotics, probiotics, or biologics in most patients. For the small minority with medically refractory disease, pouch excision with permanent ileostomy eliminates pouchitis.

faqs.question: Does diet affect pouchitis?
faqs.answer: Diet does not cause pouchitis but certain foods can worsen symptoms during a flare. A low-FODMAP diet reduces gas, bloating, and urgency by limiting fermentable carbohydrates. High-fibre foods and spicy meals may increase output. Staying well hydrated is important since pouch patients lack a colon to reabsorb water.

faqs.question: Is pouchitis related to ulcerative colitis?
faqs.answer: Closely. Pouchitis occurs almost exclusively in people who had their colon removed for ulcerative colitis. Those with familial adenomatous polyposis (FAP) who have the same surgery develop pouchitis in under 5% of cases. This suggests the underlying inflammatory biology of UC continues to affect the pouch mucosa even after the colon is gone.

faqs.question: What is cuffitis and how is it different from pouchitis?
faqs.answer: Cuffitis is inflammation of the small residual strip of rectal mucosa (the cuff) left just below the pouch-anal anastomosis. Its symptoms overlap with pouchitis but it does not respond to antibiotics; it requires topical mesalamine suppositories or steroid foam. Pouchoscopy differentiates them by showing normal pouch but inflamed cuff.

faqs.question: Can pouchitis require my pouch to be removed?
faqs.answer: Yes, in approximately 5-10% of IPAA patients. Indications include chronic antibiotic-refractory pouchitis unresponsive to all therapies, Crohn's disease of the pouch with fistulae or strictures, pouch ischaemia, and persistent pouch failure. Pouch excision with permanent ileostomy eliminates the complication and many patients report improved quality of life after removal.

faqs.question: How common is pouchitis?
faqs.answer: Very common among IPAA patients. The cumulative incidence reaches approximately 50% at 10 years after surgery. Annual incidence is 7-20% per pouch-year, making pouchitis more likely than not over the life of the pouch. It is the single most common long-term complication of IPAA surgery.

faqs.question: What is faecal microbiota transplantation for pouchitis?
faqs.answer: FMT involves transferring stool from a healthy screened donor into the pouch via endoscopy to restore a normal microbial balance. Small trials show it modestly improves antibiotic-refractory pouchitis in some patients by increasing microbiome diversity. It is not yet standard of care and is mainly used in specialist centres under research protocols.

metaDescription: Pouchitis is inflammation of the ileal pouch after colectomy for ulcerative colitis. Learn symptoms, antibiotic and biologic treatment. Doctor-reviewed.
